Reverse-engineer the ARCHITECTURE of: **openai-deepresearch (47.84) AND gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch (49.98, the reference) — the two systems we can most directly calibrate against**

THE LEADERBOARD ON OUR EVALUATOR (GPT-5.5) — this is the ONLY board that matters to us:
  cellcog-max 55.78 | WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78 | bodhi 54.07 | lunon 53.51 | dalpha-deepresearch 53.10
  sourcery 51.17 | gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98 (== the "reference", so parity = 0.50) | openai-deepresearch 47.84
  POLARIS (us) 43.82 | perplexity-Research 43.05 | grok-deeper-search 41.22
NOTE: the widely-cited "ADORE 52.65" is on NEITHER board — treat it as unverified. The legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro
board (qianfan 58.03, ZTE 57.27, ...) is a DIFFERENT evaluator and its numbers are NOT comparable to ours.
We already hold 100-task artifacts for: cellcog, tavily, onyx, deepinsight, baidu-qianfan (in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus).

DO BOTH of these:
1. THE PUBLISHED RECORD. Search hard: arXiv, GitHub, company blog, HF model/dataset card, tech report, WeChat/Zhihu
   posts (several of these are Chinese teams — search Chinese-language sources too: 深度研究, 智能体, 报告生成).
   Is there a paper? Code? A tech report? If there is genuinely NOTHING public, SAY SO — do not invent an architecture.
2. THE ARTIFACT. If we have its reports in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus (or the Acquire phase just fetched them), READ THEM and measure
   them across MANY tasks, not just task 72. Reverse-engineer the generation process from the text: section/subsection
   structure, paragraph length distribution, whether it uses bullets/tables (and whether that varies BY TASK GENRE),
   how it cites, whether it names studies in prose, how it handles conflicting evidence, whether it has a dedicated
   synthesis/tensions section, whether the writing looks single-pass or multi-step, whether there is evidence of a
   revision/critique pass.

THE QUESTION THAT MATTERS MOST: **how does it COMPOSE the report, and how does it produce SYNTHESIS?**
We are an extraction-grounded fact conveyor — we span-verify every sentence, which forbids cross-source inference.
We score insight 0.4238 (weight 0.32, our worst). Find out what these systems do INSTEAD.

Prefer primary sources. Mark anything unverified. We were burned tonight by a confidently-quoted stale number.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## DeepResearch Bench Overview

DeepResearch Bench is a benchmark consisting of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields. The RACE (Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation framework with Dynamic Weighting) framework is used for evaluating the quality of generated research reports.

## RACE Framework Results

Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrated leading overall performance (48.88) across all dimensions, with OpenAI Deep Research following, according to the initial results. However, more recent evaluations show shifting rankings. Felo Research achieved an Overall Score of 0.4937 (~49.37/100) on DeepResearch Bench — placing it ahead of leading competitors.

OpenAI Deep Research achieved the highest score in Instruction-Following (49.27), indicating that evaluation dimensions capture distinct capabilities.

## Key Finding

Deep Research Agents significantly outperformed LLMs with Search Tools in terms of Effective Citations, with Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research achieving an exceptional 111.21 average effective citations, demonstrating superior information gathering capabilities.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about OpenAI's Deep Research architecture and o3:

## Architecture Overview

OpenAI's Deep Research (DR) capability employs a single-agent architecture centered around a reinforcement learning-based, fine-tuned o3 reasoning model. Deep Research is an agent-like AI model originally built on OpenAI's o3 reasoning model, specifically trained for autonomous research tasks.

## Key Components

Upon receiving a research query, the system initiates a concise interactive clarification step to accurately define user intent and research objectives, then autonomously formulates and executes a sophisticated, multi-step research strategy, encompassing multimodal information retrieval, web browsing, and computational tasks such as data analysis and visualization through browser tools.

## Training Approach

During training, OpenAI used end-to-end reinforcement learning on complex browsing and reasoning tasks, with the model placed in simulated research environments with access to tools and given real-world tasks requiring multi-step problem solving. OpenAI developed Deep Research using the same "chain of thought" reinforcement-learning methods it used to create its o1 multistep reasoning model.

## Capabilities

Through reinforcement learning-optimized task scheduling within a unified agent framework, the system demonstrates enhanced autonomous planning and adaptive execution capabilities, enabling rapid completion of complex research workflows. In response to a single query, Deep Research will search the web, analyze the information it encounters, and compile a detailed report that cites its sources.

import json,re,statistics as st
from collections import Counter
def load(f):
    return {json.loads(l)['id']: json.loads(l) for l in open(f)}
sets={'openai':load('gpt55_board/openai-deepresearch.jsonl'),
      'gemini':load('gpt55_board/gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch.jsonl')}
for name,d in sets.items():
    W=[];H1=[];H2=[];H3=[];H4=[];PAR=[];BUL=[];TBL=[];TBLROWS=[];CIT=[];UNIQCIT=[];SECW=[]
    refsec=0; execsum=0; tensions=0; numsec=0
    inline_url=0; footnote=0
    for i,r in d.items():
        a=r['article']; w=len(a.split()); W.append(w)
        lines=a.split('\n')
        h1=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^# ',l)]; h2=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^## ',l)]
        h3=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^### ',l)]; h4=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^#### ',l)]
        H1.append(len(h1));H2.append(len(h2));H3.append(len(h3));H4.append(len(h4))
        bul=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',l)]); BUL.append(bul)
        tbl=len(re.findall(r'^\|[-: |]+\|$',a,re.M)); TBL.append(tbl)
        TBLROWS.append(len([l for l in lines if l.strip().startswith('|')]))
        paras=[p for p in re.split(r'\n\s*\n',a) if not p.strip().startswith(('#','|','-','*')) and len(p.split())>15]
        PAR += [len(p.split()) for p in paras]
        SECW.append(w/max(1,len(h2)))
        urls=re.findall(r'https?://[^\s\)\]]+',a); CIT.append(len(urls)); UNIQCIT.append(len(set(urls)))
        low=a.lower()
        if re.search(r'^#+.*(works cited|references|bibliography|sources)',a,re.M|re.I): refsec+=1
        if re.search(r'^#+.*(executive summary|abstract|introduction|overview)',a,re.M|re.I): execsum+=1
        if re.search(r'^#+.*\d+[\.\)]\s',a,re.M): numsec+=1
        if re.search(r'(tension|conflicting|contradict|discrepan|caveat|counterpoint|however, .{0,40}argu|debate)',low): tensions+=1
        if re.search(r'\[\d+\]',a): footnote+=1
        if re.search(r'\[[^\]]+\]\(https?://',a): inline_url+=1
    n=len(d)
    p=lambda x: f"{st.mean(x):.1f} (med {st.median(x):.0f}, min {min(x)}, max {max(x)})"
    print(f"=== {name} n={n}")
    print(" words     :",p(W))
    print(" H1/H2/H3/H4:",f"{st.mean(H1):.1f}/{st.mean(H2):.1f}/{st.mean(H3):.1f}/{st.mean(H4):.1f}")
    print(" words per H2 sec:",f"{st.mean(SECW):.0f}")
    print(" para words:",f"mean {st.mean(PAR):.0f} med {st.median(PAR):.0f} n={len(PAR)} p90={sorted(PAR)[int(.9*len(PAR))]}")
    print(" bullet lines:",p(BUL))
    print(" tables:",p(TBL), " table lines:",f"{st.mean(TBLROWS):.0f}")
    print(" urls:",p(CIT)," uniq:",f"{st.mean(UNIQCIT):.0f}")
    print(f" refs-section {refsec}/{n}  exec-summary {execsum}/{n}  numbered-headings {numsec}/{n}  tension-lang {tensions}/{n}")
    print(f" markdown-link-cites {inline_url}/{n}  [n]-footnote {footnote}/{n}")
    print(" reports with 0 tables:",sum(1 for t in TBL if t==0), " with 0 bullets:",sum(1 for b in BUL if b==0))
EOF

The Gemini Deep Research Agent autonomously plans, executes, and synthesizes multi-step research tasks, powered by Gemini, navigating complex information landscapes to produce detailed, cited reports. A single request triggers an autonomous loop of planning, searching, reading, and reasoning.

# Deep Research Agent Architecture

## Core Process Flow

The documentation describes Deep Research as a **multi-step agentic workflow** rather than a simple generation model. The key architectural phases are:

**Planning & Execution Loop:**
> "The Gemini Deep Research Agent autonomously plans, executes, and synthesizes multi-step research tasks."

> "Plan -> Search -> Read -> Iterate -> Output"

This contrasts with standard models that use "Generate -> Output."

## Iterative Research Methodology

The agent operates through **autonomous cycles** of information gathering and refinement:

> "Research tasks involve iterative searching and reading and can take several minutes to complete."

The system allows collaborative intervention in the planning phase before execution begins, enabling users to review and modify the research direction.

## Gap Identification & Refinement

The architecture supports **multi-turn refinement** through a collaborative planning mode where users can:
- Request an initial research plan
- Iterate on that plan with specific constraints
- Approve execution after refinement

## Report Synthesis

Output structuring occurs through **explicit formatting instructions**. Users can direct the agent to organize findings into specific sections, include data tables, and adjust presentation tone for different audiences.

## Intermediate Reasoning

The agent exposes thinking processes through streaming capabilities when `thinking_summaries` is enabled, providing visibility into intermediate reasoning steps during the research cycle.

The architecture emphasizes **autonomous iteration** with optional human guidance rather than single-pass generation.

## Paper Details

content.content:
Deep Research System Card
OpenAI∗
February 25, 2025
1 Introduction
Deep research is a new agentic capability that conducts multi-step research on the internet for
complex tasks. The deep research model is powered by an early version of OpenAI o3 that is
optimized for web browsing. Deep research leverages reasoning to search, interpret, and analyze
massive amounts of text, images, and PDFs on the internet, pivoting as needed in reaction to
information it encounters. It can also read files provided by the user and analyze data by writing
and executing python code. We believe deep research will be useful to people across a wide range
of situations.
Before launching deep research and making it available to our Pro users, we conducted rigorous
safety testing, Preparedness evaluations and governance reviews. We also ran additional safety
testing to better understand incremental risks associated with deep research’s ability to browse
the web, and added new mitigations. Key areas of new work included strengthening privacy
protections around personal information that is published online, and training the model to resist
malicious instructions that it may come across while searching the Internet.
At the same time, our testing on deep research also surfaced opportunities to further improve our
testing methods. We took the time before broadening the release of deep research to conduct
further human probing and automated testing for select risks.
Building on OpenAI’s established safety practices and Preparedness Framework, this system card
provides more details on how we built deep research, learned about its capabilities and risks, and
improved safety prior to launch.
2 Model data and training
Deep research was trained on new browsing datasets created specifically for research use cases.
The model learned the core browsing capabilities (searching, clicking, scrolling, interpreting files),
how to use a python tool in a sandboxed setting (for carrying out calculations, doing data analysis
and plotting graphs), and how to reason through and synthesize a large number of websites to
find specific pieces of information or write comprehensive reports through reinforcement learning
training on these browsing tasks.
∗Please cite this work as “OpenAI (2025)”.
1
The training datasets contain a range of tasks from objective auto-gradable tasks with ground truth
answers, to more open-ended tasks with accompanying rubrics for grading. During training, the
model responses are graded against the ground truth answers or rubrics using a chain-of-thought
model as a grader.
The model was also trained on existing safety datasets re-used from OpenAI o1 training, as well
as some new, browsing-specific safety datasets created for deep research.
3 Risk identification, assessment and mitigation
3.1 External red teaming methodology
OpenAI worked with groups of external red teamers to assess key risks associated with the
capabilities of deep research. External red teaming focused on risk areas including personal
information and privacy, disallowed content, regulated advice, dangerous advice, and risky advice.
We also asked red teamers to test more general approaches to circumventing the model’s safeguards,
including prompt injections and jailbreaks.
Red teamers were able to circumvent some refusals with targeted jailbreaks and adversarial

import json,re,statistics as st
g=[json.loads(l) for l in open('gpt55_board/gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch.jsonl')]
mono=[];reuse=[];firstpos=[]
for r in g:
    a=r['article']
    parts=re.split(r'^#+\s*\**\s*(?:引用的著作|works cited|参考文献)',a,flags=re.M|re.I)
    if len(parts)<2: continue
    b=parts[0]
    # citation tokens: digits attached after a word char/punct
    toks=[(m.start(),int(m.group(1))) for m in re.finditer(r'(?<=[a-zA-Z\)\]”"\.,%])\s?(\d{1,3})(?=[\s\.,;:\)]|$)',b)]
    if len(toks)<20: continue
    seq=[n for _,n in toks]
    first={}
    for i,(p,n) in enumerate(toks):
        if n not in first: first[n]=i
    order=[first[n] for n in sorted(first)]
    # spearman-ish: fraction of adjacent pairs in increasing first-appearance
    inc=sum(1 for i in range(len(order)-1) if order[i]<order[i+1])/max(1,len(order)-1)
    mono.append(inc)
    reuse.append(len(seq)/len(set(seq)))
print("gemini n=%d docs"%len(mono))
print("  citation-number first-appearance monotonicity: mean %.2f (0.5=random, 1.0=perfectly linear)"%st.mean(mono))

input.architecture:
TWO GENUINELY DIFFERENT ARCHITECTURES. Not variants of one design. Artifacts confirm the published descriptions.

== OpenAI DR: ONE MODEL, ONE ROLLOUT, NO PIPELINE ==
Deep Research System Card (OpenAI, 2025-02-25), sections 1-2, verbatim:
- "The deep research model is powered by an early version of OpenAI o3 that is optimized for web browsing."
- "The model learned the core browsing capabilities (searching, clicking, scrolling, interpreting files), how to use a python tool in a sandboxed setting ... and how to reason through and synthesize a large number of websites to find specific pieces of information or write comprehensive reports through reinforcement learning training on these browsing tasks."
- Training data ranged from "objective auto-gradable tasks with ground truth answers, to more open-ended tasks with accompanying rubrics for grading ... graded against the ground truth answers or rubrics using a chain-of-thought model as a grader."

So: NO planner module, NO separate writer, NO critic, NO outline artifact. Report-writing is an ACTION inside a single RL-trained agentic rollout. Report quality is baked into the WEIGHTS by rubric-graded RL (an LLM judge in the TRAINING loop, not at inference). Brief clarifying-question turn, then one continuous browse-reason loop, then the report is emitted from whatever is in context. All the machinery is at train time; nothing at inference time. This is why it is NOT copyable by us: the lever is RL, not scaffolding.

== Gemini 2.5 Pro DR: EXPLICIT PLAN -> ITERATE -> SELF-CRITIQUE PIPELINE ==
Google primary sources:
- ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/deep-research documents the loop literally as "Plan -> Search -> Read -> Iterate -> Output", explicitly contrasted against a normal model's "Generate -> Output". "The Gemini Deep Research Agent autonomously plans, executes, and synthesizes multi-step research tasks."
- gemini.google/overview/deep-research/ (THE load-bearing sentence): "Gemini critically evaluates the information, identifies key themes and inconsistencies, and structures the report in a logical and informative way, even performing multiple passes of self-critique to enhance clarity and detail."
- blog.google (Dec 2024 launch): "it creates a multi-step research plan for you to either revise or approve"; the loop is "searching, finding interesting pieces of information and then starting a new search based on what it has learned. It repeats this process multiple times"; and it leans on "the Gemini model's advanced reasoning capabilities and our 1M token context window".

So Gemini has, at INFERENCE time, three things OpenAI does not: (1) an explicit user-visible research PLAN produced before any browsing; (2) an explicit gap/discrepancy-driven iteration loop; (3) a REVISION LOOP over the drafted report ("multiple passes of self-critique"). And the writer sees the ENTIRE retrieved corpus at once in a 1M window.

== ARTIFACT FORENSICS (100 tasks each, our own JSONL) ==
Composition unit. Gemini outlines are DERIVED ANALYTIC FRAMES, not question mirrors. Task 51 (Japan elderly market) Gemini outline: 1. Executive Summary / 2. Evolving Landscape (2.1 Demographic Projections, 2.2 Deep Dive into Aging Trends, 2.3 Regional Dimensions) / 3. Economic Profile (3.1 Income, 3.2 Wealth, 3.3 Debt, 3.4 Critical Economic Factors) / 4. Current Consumption / 5. Future Trajectories / 6. Market Size Forecasts (6.1 Forecasting Approach ... 6.6 Discussion of Key Assumptions and Potential Variations) / 7. Strategic Implications and Conclusion (7.1 Quantifying the Longevity Economy Opportunity, 7.2 Navigating Challenges, 7.3 Recommendations, 7.4 Concluding Remarks). Note 6.1 and 6.6: it BUILDS a projection model and states its assumptions and their fragility. OpenAI on the same task: seven flat H2s that just mirror the question's own list (Food / Housing / Clothing / Transportation / Summary). No exec summary, no methodology, no implications section.

Structural spread (mean over 100):
- words: gemini 6727 (median 5705) vs openai 4743 (median 3192)
- H3-per-H2 (hierarchical decomposition): gemini 1.48 vs openai 0.52
- H3 count: gemini 11.4 vs openai 2.8
- tables: gemini 2.5/report, 95/100 reports have >=1 table; openai 0.5, only 35/100 have one
- bullet lines: gemini 187 vs openai 28 (but gemini bullets are ATOMIC: median 15 words; openai bullets are FAT: median 51, p90 202 -- openai's bullets ARE the content, gemini's are enumerated evidence hung off prose)
- unique URLs cited: gemini 113 vs openai 43
- headings containing implications/synthesis/strategic: gemini 29/100 vs openai 5/100
- headings containing limitation/caveat/uncertainty/risk: gemini 36/100 vs openai 11/100
- headings containing methodology/approach/assumptions: gemini 33/100 vs openai 18/100
- executive summary section: gemini 14/100, openai 0/100
- "taken together / collectively / synthesis / when combined" per 1k words: gemini 0.28 vs openai 0.08

Genre conditioning: Gemini scales DEPTH by genre (H3: how-to 22.8, compare 12.3, market 10.8, science 9.8; words: how-to 10950, market 9392, science 7470) but its TABLE use is genre-INVARIANT (a table appears in essentially every report regardless of genre). Tables are a house style, not a genre adaptation. OpenAI scales length similarly but never adds structure.

Citation mechanics differ completely. OpenAI: inline markdown links in the body, no bibliography (only 8/100 have a references section), 99/100 use [text](url). Gemini: bare superscript-style numerals glued to the text ("...robotics 3, a growing emphasis on health 20, and lifestyle shifts...11"), resolved by a global numbered "Works cited" list at the end (avg 113 unique sources).

Forensic test for one-pass vs. section-scoped writing (Gemini): I measured whether citation NUMBERS increase monotonically with first appearance in the body. Monotonicity = 0.63 (1.0 = numbering assigned by order of first use, 0.5 = random). It is only weakly linear => the numbered source list is a PRE-EXISTING enumeration of the retrieved corpus, and the writer cites INTO it from any point in the document. Combined with source reuse of 4.67 cite-tokens per distinct source, and 30% of sources appearing in MORE THAN ONE H2 section, this is GLOBAL-CONTEXT composition over a fully-retrieved corpus, not per-section RAG. A source is never "owned" by a section.
input.composition_method:
THE ANSWER TO THE QUESTION THAT MATTERS.

== GEMINI: retrieve-everything, then write ONE document against a THEME-derived outline, then self-critique ==
Sequence, as best reconstructed:
1. PLAN: an explicit multi-point research plan is emitted BEFORE any retrieval (user can edit it). The plan is over QUESTIONS/THEMES, not over sources.
2. ITERATE: browse -> read -> "identifies missing information and discrepancies to explore" -> browse again. Loop is gap-driven. It terminates when the model judges coverage sufficient. Result: ~113 distinct sources, all held in a 1M-token window.
3. OUTLINE FROM THEMES, NOT SOURCES: "identifies key themes and inconsistencies, and structures the report in a logical and informative way". The section tree is an ANALYTIC frame invented after reading (see task 51: "Forecasting Approach", "Discussion of Key Assumptions", "Navigating Challenges"). It routinely contains sections that exist NOWHERE in the user's question.
4. SINGLE GLOBAL-CONTEXT WRITE: one writer, whole corpus in context, whole outline in context. Evidence: citation numbers do not increase monotonically (0.63), sources are reused ~4.7x and cross 2+ sections 30% of the time. Section-scoped writers cannot do that.
5. REVISION: "multiple passes of self-critique to enhance clarity and detail" (Google's words). This is a real, documented critic loop at INFERENCE time.

== HOW SYNTHESIS IS ACTUALLY PRODUCED (the paragraph-level mechanism) ==
This is the finding that matters most for POLARIS. Gemini's insight is NOT produced by "writing unsourced opinion". Measure this:

  sentence-level UNCITED rate:   gemini 37.6%   openai 83.1%
  paragraphs where EVERY sentence is cited:  gemini 17.4%  openai 0.7%
  paragraphs where NO sentence is cited:     gemini 15.8%  openai 41.2%

Gemini is BOTH more densely grounded AND more insightful (insight 0.4992 vs 0.4669). OpenAI writes four times as much unsupported prose and scores LOWER on insight. So: uncited prose is NOT insight. This kills the naive "just let the model opine" fix.

What Gemini actually does is a CLAIM-EVIDENCE-WARRANT paragraph. Uncited-sentence rate by position within a paragraph: pos0 41.0%, pos1 35.0%, pos2 34.3%, pos3 34.5%, pos4(last) 38.8%. The paragraph OPENS with an uncited analytic claim, fills the middle with densely-cited evidence FROM MULTIPLE SOURCES, and CLOSES with an uncited inference. 25.8% of its multi-sentence paragraphs are exactly "cited body + uncited synthesis coda" (and those paragraphs are 28% likely to carry tension/trade-off/implication language vs 18% elsewhere).

Canonical example, verbatim, gemini task 63 (lithium niobate etching):
"The inherent characteristics of physical versus reactive etching methods present a fundamental challenge in simultaneously achieving both smooth and vertical sidewalls. Ar+ sputtering, being a physical process, tends to produce smoother surfaces due to its polishing effect [22] but lacks the chemical selectivity needed for high anisotropy, resulting in sloped sidewalls [22]. Conversely, reactive etching chemistries (e.g., fluorine-based) can offer better directionality and steeper profiles [22] but frequently suffer from the redeposition of non-volatile reaction byproducts like LiF, which inevitably leads to increased sidewall roughness.[1] This inherent conflict necessitates careful process selection and optimization, often involving trade-offs based on whether sidewall angle or smoothness is the more critical parameter."

Sentence 1: UNCITED analytic frame ("a fundamental challenge"). Sentences 2-3: CITED evidence from two different sources, deliberately juxtaposed. Sentence 4: UNCITED warrant that names the trade-off. Our span-verifier would delete sentence 1 and sentence 4 -- i.e. exactly the two sentences that carry the insight -- and leave a fact list.

Conflict handling is a first-class move, not an accident. Gemini task 53 reconciles rather than picks: it reports ADIA AUM at 708B [18] AND 1.11T [19], Norway GPFG at 1.34T [18] AND 1.74T [10], then adds the uncited meta-claim that "Different reporting organizations (SWF Institute, Global SWF, IFSWF) may use slightly different methodologies ... leading to discrepancies" and "These variations underscore the challenge of obtaining precise, real-time data for funds with lower transparency." It turns a citation CONFLICT into an INSIGHT about the domain. 44/100 Gemini reports contain explicit tension/conflict/discrepancy language vs 32/100 for OpenAI, and Gemini gives it a HEADING 42/100 times vs 14/100.

== OPENAI: no composition method at all, in the scaffolding sense ==
The report is the terminal emission of one RL rollout. It mirrors the question's structure, is flat (0.52 H3 per H2), has no executive summary in ANY of 100 reports, rarely tables, and is 83% uncited prose. Its instruction-following (49.29) is its best dimension precisely because it echoes the prompt's own shape. Its insight (46.69) is its worst. It is what our system would look like if we removed the span-verifier and let the model ramble: fluent, unsourced, structurally lazy.

== NEGATIVE RESULT WORTH FLAGGING (relevant to your recent length work) ==
38/100 OpenAI reports are under 1500 words. Their mean overall is 0.471 vs 0.483 for the other 62 -- a gap of only 1.2 points. Length is NOT what separates the two systems. Gemini's +2.14 overall and +3.23 insight over OpenAI is bought by ARGUMENT STRUCTURE (theme-derived outline, claim-evidence-warrant paragraphs, an explicit tensions/implications/limits section, a self-critique pass), not by word count. Chasing 8000 words will not move insight.

input.performance:
On OUR GPT-5.5 board:
  gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98 = comp 50.01 / insight 49.92 / IF 50.22 / read 49.58. It IS the reference report, so it sits at ~0.50 on every dimension by construction. Parity with it = 0.50 everywhere. It is the CALIBRATION ZERO, not a competitor -- the six systems above it (cellcog-max 55.78 down to sourcery 51.17) all beat the reference.
  openai-deepresearch 47.84 = comp 48.05 / insight 46.69 / IF 49.29 / read 47.62.

How they differ from the pack:
- OpenAI is the ONLY strong system whose best dimension is instruction-following (49.29) and whose worst is insight (46.69). It mirrors the prompt's structure and does not add an analytic frame. It is 2.14 BELOW the reference overall and 3.23 below on insight -- its insight deficit is 1.5x its overall deficit.
- Gemini's profile is flat by definition. Its distinguishing artifact-level features vs OpenAI: 3x the H3 depth, 2.7x the sources, tables in 95/100 vs 35/100 reports, an implications/synthesis heading 6x as often, a limitations heading 3x as often, and 2.2x lower uncited-sentence rate.
- POLARIS at 43.82 sits 6.16 below the reference and 4.02 below OpenAI. Our insight 0.4238 is 7.5 points below the reference's 0.4992 -- a LARGER gap than OpenAI's. We are being beaten on insight by a system that has NO synthesis machinery at all, which means our deficit is not "we lack a critic loop", it is "our span-verifier is actively deleting the sentences that score insight".
- Everything at 51+ on this board (cellcog-max, WhaleCloud, bodhi, lunon, dalpha, sourcery) beats the reference on INSIGHT by more than it beats it on readability -- insight is the dimension the leaders are farming. Readability is the flattest column (49.68-53.20). Insight is the widest (38.12-57.08). Insight is where the board is won, and it is weight 0.32 for us.
what_we_can_steal: THE ONE THAT MATTERS -- TWO-TIER SENTENCE TYPING. Do not abandon span-verification; RE-SCOPE it. Gemini's insight sentences are not ungrounded, they are grounded in a DIFFERENT premise set. Split every sentence into (a) EVIDENCE sentences, which stay span-verified against the source corpus exactly as today, and (b) INFERENCE sentences (the analytic topic sentence and the warrant/coda), which are verified NOT against the web but against the Tier-1 evidence sentences ALREADY IN THEIR OWN PARAGRAPH -- an entailment/consistency check against local cited premises, not a retrieval check. This is fully checkable and preserves our anti-hallucination guarantee, but it legalizes cross-source inference. Right now our verifier deletes exactly the sentences that carry insight. Measured target: Gemini runs 37.6% uncited sentences with 17.4% of paragraphs fully cited; we should aim for a ~35-40% inference-sentence budget, not 0%.

CLAIM-EVIDENCE-WARRANT PARAGRAPH TEMPLATE. Enforce it in the writer prompt: sentence 1 = an uncited analytic assertion that names what this paragraph argues; middle = 2-4 cited facts drawn from AT LEAST TWO DIFFERENT SOURCES, deliberately juxtaposed with 'Conversely' / 'but' / 'whereas'; final sentence = an uncited warrant that states the trade-off, tension, or implication. Gemini's uncited-rate-by-position profile is U-shaped (41% at pos0, ~34% in the middle, 39% at the last) -- that shape IS the template. Copy it literally.

THEME-DERIVED OUTLINE, NOT QUESTION-MIRRORING OUTLINE. Build the outline AFTER retrieval, from the themes and inconsistencies found in the corpus, and require it to contain at least one section that does not appear in the user's question. Gemini's canonical tail sections: 'Forecasting Approach', 'Discussion of Key Assumptions and Potential Variations', 'Navigating Challenges', 'Strategic Implications'. OpenAI's outline is just the question's own bullet list re-headed -- and it loses. Target the measured deltas: H3-per-H2 1.48 (not 0.52), an implications/synthesis heading in ~30% of reports, a limitations/caveats heading in ~36%.

A DEDICATED TENSIONS SECTION *AND* DISTRIBUTED TENSION PARAGRAPHS. Gemini does both: 42/100 reports have a so-what/tension/limits heading, and 44/100 carry tension language inside body prose. Do not settle for a bolt-on 'Synthesis' section at the end -- the insight is distributed through the body.

TURN CITATION CONFLICTS INTO INSIGHT INSTEAD OF DROPPING THEM. When two sources disagree on a number, our extraction-grounded pipeline currently must pick one or drop both. Gemini REPORTS BOTH WITH BOTH CITATIONS and then adds an uncited meta-claim explaining WHY they disagree (different methodologies, different reporting bodies, transparency levels) -- see gemini task 53 on ADIA AUM (708B vs 1.11T). A conflict is free insight. Build an explicit 'disagreement detector' over our extracted spans and route every detected conflict into a claim-evidence-warrant paragraph.

SELF-CRITIQUE PASS AT INFERENCE TIME. Google says it plainly: 'multiple passes of self-critique to enhance clarity and detail.' We have a judge but our git log says it fell off the critical path. Put a critic back ON the path, but critique the ARGUMENT (does every section state a claim? does every paragraph end with a warrant? are conflicts surfaced?) rather than the facts -- facts are already gated by the verifier.

GLOBAL-CONTEXT COMPOSITION, NOT SECTION-SCOPED RAG. Forensics say Gemini's writer holds the whole retrieved corpus with stable source indices and reuses each source ~4.7x, with 30% of sources cited in 2+ sections. If our writer is section-scoped (each section sees only its own retrieved chunks), cross-source inference is architecturally impossible no matter what we do to the verifier. Check this first.

HOUSE-STYLE TABLES, GENRE-INVARIANT. Gemini puts >=1 table in 95/100 reports regardless of genre; OpenAI in 35/100 and scores lower on readability AND comprehensiveness. Cheap, mechanical, and it forces cross-source consolidation (a comparison table cannot be built from one source).

DO NOT CHASE LENGTH. Hard negative result: OpenAI's 38 sub-1500-word reports score 0.471 vs 0.483 for its 62 longer ones -- a 1.2 point gap. Meanwhile Gemini beats OpenAI by 3.23 on insight. Length is nearly free and nearly worthless; argument structure is the whole game. Given the recent flywheel work pushed 3408 -> 7723 words, this is worth re-reading.

DO NOT TRY TO COPY OPENAI DEEP RESEARCH. There is no architecture to copy. It is one o3 rollout whose report quality was trained in by rubric-graded RL. Its scaffolding is empty. It is also LOSING to the reference. It is a useful control, not a target.
unknowns: Gemini DR: the actual PROMPTS, the outline schema, and the number/content of the self-critique passes are not public. 'Multiple passes of self-critique' is Google marketing copy on the product page -- it is a first-party claim but the mechanism (separate critic model? same model re-reading? what rubric?) is NOT disclosed anywhere I could find. Treat the existence of a revision loop as CONFIRMED-BY-VENDOR but its implementation as UNKNOWN.

Gemini DR: whether the writer is literally one LLM call over the 1M window, or a section-by-section writer with the full corpus re-attached each time. My citation-numbering forensic (monotonicity 0.63) proves the source INDEX is global and sources are reused across sections; it does NOT prove the number of generation calls. Both are consistent with the evidence.

Gemini DR: no arXiv paper, no tech report, no model card section, no code. The Gemini 2.5 tech report (arXiv:2507.06261) does not contain a Deep Research architecture section that I could locate. The ONLY sources are three Google product/docs pages. This is genuinely thin.

OpenAI DR: the rubric used to grade open-ended report-writing tasks during RL is not published. That rubric is effectively the composition method, and it is the single most valuable undisclosed artifact in either system.
OpenAI DR: whether any post-hoc formatting/citation-insertion step runs after the rollout. Nothing in the system card says so; the flat outlines and 83% uncited prose suggest not.

The exact criteria the RACE Insight dimension rewards. I could not extract the definitions from arXiv:2506.11763 (PDF text extraction failed). I am inferring from artifact-vs-score contrast, not from the rubric. Someone should get the rubric text -- it is the highest-value single document for us and we do not currently have it verified.
Whether these 100 artifacts are the SAME generations the benchmark authors scored, or re-runs. If re-runs, the structural stats hold but the score-to-artifact mapping is looser than I assumed.
